docs.title: dev-bootstrap
docs.text: dev-bootstrap discovers repositories from GitHub and GitLab, reads a small metadata file from each one, and organises everything under ~/code/ — so your local environment mirrors exactly how you structure your work.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
gh repo list / GitLab API
        ↓
  git clone --depth 1
        ↓
  read .repo-meta.yml
        ↓
  ~/code/<dev_path>/<repo-name>
  1. Discover — the CLI (or shell scripts) query GitHub via gh and/or a GitLab API.
  2. Clone — each repo is shallow-cloned into a temporary staging area.
  3. Read metadata — if the repo contains a .repo-meta.yml, its dev_path field determines the final location.
  4. Place — the repo is moved to ~/code/<dev_path>/<name>. Repos without metadata land in ~/code/misc/.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/edgar-treischl/dev-bootstrap.git
cd dev-bootstrap
poetry install

# Bootstrap all repos for a GitHub user
poetry run dev bootstrap --github-user <your-github-username>

See Installation for prerequisites and Usage for all commands.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Tool Purpose Install Python ≥ 3.14 Runtime python.org Poetry Dependency & packaging manager curl -sSL https://install.python-poetry.org \| python3 - GitHub CLI (gh) GitHub repo discovery brew install gh yq YAML parsing in shell scripts brew install yq jq JSON parsing in shell scripts brew install jq

GitLab

GitLab discovery requires a personal access token stored in the GITLAB_TOKEN environment variable — no extra CLI tool needed.

docs.title: Install dev-bootstrap
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/edgar-treischl/dev-bootstrap.git
cd dev-bootstrap
poetry install

This installs the dev command into the project's virtual environment.

docs.title: Authenticate GitHub CLI
docs.text:
gh auth login

Follow the prompts to authenticate. After this, dev bootstrap --github-user will work without any token.

docs.title: Set a GitLab token
docs.text:
export GITLAB_TOKEN=<your-personal-access-token>

Add this to your shell profile (~/.zshrc, ~/.bashrc, etc.) to make it permanent.

docs.title: Verify the installation
docs.text:
poetry run dev --help

You should see the list of available commands.

docs.title: .repo-meta.yml Schema
docs.text: Each repository can contain a .repo-meta.yml at its root. This file drives where the repo is placed locally and provides structured metadata visible via dev scan.

docs.title: Full example
docs.text:
name: my-api
description: REST API for the core platform
dev_path: work/apps        # → ~/code/work/apps/my-api
role: project
tags:
  - backend
  - python
language: python
setup:
  - pip install -e .
  - pre-commit install
env:
  - .env.example
services:
  - postgres
  - redis
depends_on:
  - my-core-lib
workspace: []
update_strategy: []
priority: []

docs.title: Field reference
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string no Display name (falls back to the git repo name) description string no One-line description of the repository dev_path string | list yes Path suffix under ~/code/. Missing or empty → misc role string no Classification: project, tool, library, service, … tags list[string] no Free-form labels for filtering / searching language string no Primary programming language setup list[string] no Commands to run after cloning (not run automatically yet) env list[string] no Environment template files (e.g. .env.example) services list[string] no External services the repo requires (postgres, redis, …) depends_on list[string] no Other repo names this one depends on workspace list no Workspace configuration (reserved) update_strategy list no Git update strategy hints (reserved) priority list no Priority hints (reserved)

docs.title: dev_path in detail
docs.text:
dev_path is the only field that affects runtime behaviour — everything else is informational.

# String form
dev_path: work/apps           # → ~/code/work/apps/<name>

# List form (joined with "/")
dev_path:
  - work
  - apps                      # → ~/code/work/apps/<name>

# Empty or missing
dev_path: []                  # → ~/code/misc/<name>

docs.title: Minimal file
docs.text:
dev_path: work/apps

That's enough to have the repo placed correctly. All other fields are optional.

docs.title: Scanning Repos
docs.text: The scan command walks ~/code/ and collects every .repo-meta.yml it finds into a single table — useful for auditing what's cloned, what metadata is missing, and how projects are organised.

docs.title: Running a scan
docs.text:
poetry run dev scan

Sample output:

 repo_path                        name        role  language  tags          dev_path
 ~/code/work/apps/my-api          my-api      project  python  backend       work/apps
 ~/code/work/apps/frontend        frontend    project  node    frontend      work/apps
 ~/code/misc/old-tool             old-tool    tool     bash                  misc

docs.title: Exporting to CSV
docs.text:
poetry run dev scan --output repos.csv

This writes the same table to repos.csv and prints a confirmation line:

Exported 3 repos → repos.csv

docs.title: DataFrame columns
docs.text:
Each row corresponds to one repo. The columns map directly to .repo-meta.yml fields, plus one extra:

Column Source Notes repo_path filesystem Absolute path to the repo directory name .repo-meta.yml Repository name description .repo-meta.yml Short description dev_path .repo-meta.yml Subdirectory under ~/code/ role .repo-meta.yml e.g. project, tool, library tags .repo-meta.yml Comma-separated list language .repo-meta.yml Primary language setup .repo-meta.yml Setup commands, comma-separated env .repo-meta.yml Environment files, comma-separated services .repo-meta.yml Required services, comma-separated depends_on .repo-meta.yml Repo dependencies, comma-separated

List fields

YAML list fields are flattened to comma-separated strings so the DataFrame stays flat and CSV-exportable.

docs.title: Using the function directly
docs.text:
scan_repos() is importable for use in your own scripts or notebooks:

from devbootstrap.bootstrap import scan_repos
from pathlib import Path

# Scan the default ~/code directory
df = scan_repos()

# Or scan a specific directory
df = scan_repos(base=Path("/tmp/my-repos"))

print(df.head())
df.to_csv("repos.csv", index=False)

docs.title: Shell Scripts
docs.text: The shell/ directory contains the original Bash proof-of-concept scripts that the Python CLI is built on top of. They can be used standalone if preferred.

docs.title: bootstrap.sh
docs.text:
Entry point. Creates ~/code/ and calls clone-repos.sh.

cd shell/
./bootstrap.sh

Dependencies: clone-repos.sh, gh-discovery.sh

docs.title: clone-repos.sh
docs.text:
Orchestrates the full bootstrap flow for GitHub repos:

  1. Calls gh-discovery.sh to get a list of SSH URLs
  2. Shallow-clones each repo into ~/code/_tmp/
  3. Reads .repo-meta.yml with yq to extract dev_path
  4. Moves the repo to ~/code/<dev_path>/<name>

Dependencies: gh, git, yq

Note

GitLab support is commented out in clone-repos.sh. Use the Python CLI (poetry run dev bootstrap) for combined GitHub + GitLab discovery.

docs.title: gh-discovery.sh
docs.text:
Lists SSH URLs for all repos of a GitHub user or organisation using the gh CLI.

./gh-discovery.sh

Edit the USER_OR_ORG variable at the top of the script to change the target account. Output is one SSH URL per line.

Dependencies: gh (authenticated)

docs.title: gl-discovery.sh
docs.text:
Lists SSH URLs for all GitLab projects you are a member of.

export GITLAB_TOKEN=<your-token>
./gl-discovery.sh

Edit the GITLAB_TOKEN export and the base URL inside the script if you're targeting a self-hosted GitLab instance.

Dependencies: curl, jq

docs.title: update-all.sh
docs.text:
Finds every .git directory under ~/code/, unshallows the clone if needed, and pulls the latest changes.

./update-all.sh

Equivalent to poetry run dev update.

Dependencies: git

docs.title: Script vs CLI comparison
docs.text: Task Shell script Python CLI Bootstrap from GitHub ./bootstrap.sh poetry run dev bootstrap --github-user <user> Bootstrap from GitLab edit gl-discovery.sh poetry run dev bootstrap --gitlab-token <token> Bootstrap from both manual combination poetry run dev bootstrap --github-user <user> --gitlab-token <token> Update all repos ./update-all.sh poetry run dev update Scan metadata — poetry run dev scan

docs.title: CLI Commands
docs.text:
All commands are run through the dev entry point inside the Poetry environment:

poetry run dev <command> [OPTIONS]

docs.title: bootstrap
docs.text:
Discovers repos from GitHub and/or GitLab, shallow-clones them, and places each one under ~/code/<dev_path>/.

poetry run dev bootstrap [OPTIONS]
Option Default Description --github-user, -g — GitHub username or organisation to discover repos from --gitlab-token $GITLAB_TOKEN GitLab private token (overrides the environment variable) --gitlab-url https://gitlab.com GitLab instance base URL

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
GitHub onlyGitLab onlyBoth at once 
poetry run dev bootstrap --github-user alice

export GITLAB_TOKEN=glpat-xxxxxxxxxxxx
poetry run dev bootstrap --gitlab-url https://gitlab.example.com

poetry run dev bootstrap \
  --github-user alice \
  --gitlab-token glpat-xxxx \
  --gitlab-url https://gitlab.example.com

Where do repos land?

Each repo's .repo-meta.yml controls its final path. See .repo-meta.yml Schema for details.

docs.title: update
docs.text:
Fetches full history (unshallows) and pulls every repo already present under ~/code/.

poetry run dev update

No options. Walks all .git directories under ~/code and runs:

git fetch --unshallow
git pull --ff-only

docs.title: scan
docs.text:
Scans ~/code/ for .repo-meta.yml files, loads them into a DataFrame, and prints a summary table.

poetry run dev scan [OPTIONS]
Option Default Description --output, -o — Export the result as a CSV file to the given path

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Display tableExport to CSV 
poetry run dev scan

poetry run dev scan --output repos.csv

See Scanning Repos for more detail on the output format.

docs.title: Help
docs.text:
poetry run dev --help
poetry run dev bootstrap --help
poetry run dev scan --help
